text: Newborn Danger Signs: CHW Job Aid

The first 7 days of life are the most dangerous for a newborn baby. Most newborn deaths happen in the first 24 hours. As a CHW, visiting the home in the first day and first week of life can save lives. Teach the mother and family what to watch for. If you see any danger sign, refer to the health centre right away.
First Actions at Every Newborn Visit

When you visit a newborn: (1) Look at the baby — is the baby awake and active? (2) Check the colour — is the skin and lips pink? (3) Watch the breathing — is it regular and not too fast? (4) Ask if the baby is feeding — is the baby feeding at least 8 times in 24 hours? (5) Check the cord — does it look clean and dry? (6) Check temperature — is the baby warm, not cold?
Danger Sign 1: Not Breathing, or Breathing Very Badly

If the baby is not breathing at all, is gasping, or the chest is pulling in very hard with each breath, this is an emergency. Count the breaths — if more than 60 breaths per minute, the baby is working too hard to breathe.
⚠️ REFER IMMEDIATELY — THIS IS AN EMERGENCY. Keep the baby warm. Ask if a health worker is nearby who knows how to help a baby breathe. Go to the health centre as fast as possible.
Danger Sign 2: Blue or White Colour (Not Pink)

A healthy baby's skin and lips are pink. If the lips and tongue are blue, or if the baby's skin is very pale or white, the baby is not getting enough oxygen. This is serious. Do not wait to see if the colour comes back.
⚠️ REFER NOW. Keep the baby warm and with the mother skin-to-skin during transport. Go to the health centre right away.
Danger Sign 3: Not Feeding or Feeding Very Poorly

A healthy newborn should breastfeed at least 8 times in 24 hours. If the baby refuses to latch on, is too weak to suck, or has not fed for more than 3 hours, this is a warning sign. A baby who cannot feed will become weak and sick very quickly.
⚠️ REFER TODAY if the baby has not fed in 3 or more hours, is very weak, or the mother says feeding is very difficult. Tell the health worker the baby is not feeding well.
Danger Sign 4: Fever or Feeling Very Cold

Feel the baby's belly, chest, and feet. A baby with fever (body feels very hot, temperature above 37.5°C) or a baby that is very cold (temperature below 36°C, feels cold to the touch, hands and feet are blue) both need urgent care. Babies cannot control their own temperature well.

⚠️ REFER NOW. While going to the health centre: if the baby is cold, put the baby skin-to-skin on the mother's chest and wrap with a blanket. If the baby has fever, keep the baby uncovered enough to stay comfortable but not cold.
Danger Sign 5: Fits or Shaking (Seizures)

If the baby shakes, stiffens, or has eyes rolling — or seems to be staring blankly while the body twitches — this is a seizure. Seizures in a newborn are very serious and can mean infection in the brain (meningitis) or lack of oxygen during birth.
⚠️ REFER IMMEDIATELY — THIS IS AN EMERGENCY. Do not put anything in the baby's mouth. Keep the baby on his or her side. Go to the health centre as fast as possible.
Danger Sign 6: Cord Looks Red, Smells Bad, or Has Pus

The cord stump (the bit of cord that stays after cutting) should fall off in 7–10 days. It should look dry and clean. If the skin around the cord is red, swollen, warm, smells bad, or has yellow or green liquid coming from it, this means infection. Cord infection can spread to the whole body very quickly in a newborn.
⚠️ REFER TODAY. While waiting or during transport: keep the cord clean and dry. Do not put any home substances (oil, ash, animal dung) on the cord. Tell the health worker: 'The cord looks infected.'
Danger Sign 7: Baby Is Yellow (Jaundice)

Some yellowing of the skin in the first few days of life is common and usually harmless. BUT: if the yellow colour is very strong, if the whites of the eyes are yellow, if the yellow spreads to the belly and legs, or if it starts in the first 24 hours of life — this needs to be checked at the health centre.
⚠️ REFER TODAY if the jaundice is strong, widespread, or starts on the first day of life. The baby may need treatment (light therapy). Severe jaundice can cause brain damage if not treated.
Supporting Breastfeeding

Breastfeeding is the best protection for a newborn. Breast milk has everything the baby needs and protects against infection. Start breastfeeding within 1 hour of birth. Do not give water, tea, or other food in the first 6 months. Feed on demand — every time the baby seems hungry, day and night.

Signs of good breastfeeding: you can hear the baby swallowing, the baby's cheeks are full (not hollow), the baby seems satisfied after feeding, and the mother does not have severe pain. If breastfeeding is very painful or the baby is not gaining weight, refer to the health centre for breastfeeding support.
Keeping the Newborn Warm

Newborns lose heat very fast. Cold kills newborns. Keep the baby dry and wrapped. The best way to keep a baby warm is skin-to-skin contact with the mother (Kangaroo Mother Care) — put the baby on the mother's chest, skin to skin, and cover both with a blanket. This works even better than a heated room or hot water bottle.
⚠️ REFER NOW if the baby is cold and does not warm up with skin-to-skin contact after 30 minutes.
